title: Greek Lemon Potatoes
subtitle: Potatoes roasted in lemon, olive oil, and oregano
excerpt: Potato wedges par-boiled then roasted in a generous amount of lemon juice, olive oil, and oregano until they absorb all the liquid and turn golden at the edges. The side dish that steals the show.
sourceNotes: Greek lemon potatoes (patates lemonates) are documented in Vefa Alexiadou, Greek Cuisine (1991) and Diane Kochilas, The Glorious Foods of Greece (2001). The key technique is adding liquid to the roasting tin so the potatoes partly absorb the lemony stock as it reduces.

content.text: Greek lemon potatoes are not roast potatoes in the British sense, there is no fat in which they crisp, no high heat to shatter the edges. Instead, they are par-cooked in a pool of olive oil and lemon juice that they absorb as it simmers down. The result is soft inside, with slightly sticky, golden edges and a flavour that is simultaneously sharp and rich.

The liquid is the key thing. Use enough so the potatoes sit partly submerged at the start, about 200 ml of lemon-stock mixture per 500 g of potato. By the time the potatoes are done, most of it will have been absorbed or evaporated, leaving a glistening, concentrated coating on each piece.

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: floury variety, peeled and cut into thick wedges

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Spread the potato wedges in a single layer in the roasting pan. They should fit without too much overlap. Whisk the olive oil, lemon juice, garlic, oregano, salt, pepper, and stock together and pour over the potatoes. The liquid should come about a third of the way up the potatoes.

Roast for 30 minutes. Turn the potatoes and spoon the pan liquid over them. Continue roasting for a further 25 minutes until the potatoes are tender when pierced, the liquid has mostly absorbed, and the cut surfaces are golden. If there is still a lot of liquid with 10 minutes to go, increase the oven to 220°C to drive it off faster.

items.fix: Add another 100 ml of water and continue roasting; the potatoes need steam to cook from inside
items.cause: Too little liquid or oven temperature too low

items.fix: Use the juice of 1 large lemon instead of 2; add a pinch of sugar to the liquid
items.cause: Variable acidity in lemons; the amount here is assertive by design

items.fix: Use a floury potato (Maris Piper) and ensure the stock proportion is correct, the liquid should include at least as much stock as oil
items.cause: Too much oil relative to liquid, or waxy potato variety
items.symptom: Potatoes are greasy rather than absorbed

content.text: Lemon potatoes are the side dish to almost every Greek main: roast chicken, roast lamb, baked fish, braised greens. They are cooked at home more often than any other potato preparation. The technique (absorbing rather than crisping) is the same principle that governs Greek braised vegetables generally, where the cooking liquid is something to be eaten, not drained away.
